coredata.dc:title: Rational design of boron-nitrogen coordinated active sites towards oxygen reduction reaction in aluminum-air batteries with robust integrated air cathode
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Ruiqi
Li, Kaiqi
Li, Zhao
Jiang, Min
Wang, Fei
Yang, Zhaohui
Zhao, Tianshuo
Meng, Pengyu
Fu, Chaopeng
coredata.dc:description: Rational design of cost-effective electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is essential for developing metal-air batteries. In this work, we propose a pre-self-assembled strategy to prepare controllable B–N coordinated active sites anchored on carbon supports as an efficient electrocatalyst for ORR. The precisely coordinated B–N coordination from the pre-self-assembled boronate-amino ligands are sealed on the in-situ formed carbon dots (CDs) for preservation and further converted into B–N coordinated active sites on the carbon support. The B–N coordinated active sites anchored on the graphene aerogel (denoted as B–N-G) exhibit Pt/C competitive ORR catalytic activity in both alkaline and neutral media, which are attributed to the high content of efficient B–N coordinated active sites with expanded lattice spacing. The operando Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and density functional theory (DFT) calculations explain the catalytic mechanism and identify the active sites of the B–N coordination. Furthermore, the B–N-G is directly processed into an integrated air cathode, and the Al-air batteries with the B–N-G integrated air cathode exhibit superior discharge performance and good stability in both NaOH and NaCl electrolytes. This study demonstrates a feasible pre-self-assembled approach to achieve designed B–N coordination in carbon-based electrocatalysts for metal-air batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B–N coordination
Integrated air cathode
Electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction
Al-air battery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) B K-edge NEXAFS spectra of the B–N-G and B-G/N-G. (b) N 1s and (c) B 1s XPS spectra of the B–N-G. (d) The fractions of N and B species in the B–N-G and B-G/N-G. (e) 11B solid-state NMR spectra of the B–N-G and B-G. (f) Solid-state FTIR of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G.

(a) SEM image, (b) TEM image, (c–f) C, B and N element mappings, and (g) HRTEM images with inserted FFT patterns of the B–N-G. (h, i) XRD patterns of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G in the whole and selected region. (j) Configuration of the B-G, N-G and B–N-G with bond lengths (unit: Å). (k) The calculated alleviated height of the B-G, N-G and B–N-G configuration.

(a) CV, (b) LSV curves and (c) Tafel slopes of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G. (d) n and HO2 − yield calculated through RRDE measurements. (e) LSV curves of the B–N-G before and after 10000 cycles. (f) Current-time chronoamperometric measurement of the B–N-G in the alkaline electrolyte. (g, h) The calculated C dl and j K, ECSA of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G in 0.1 M oxygen saturated KOH electrolyte. (i) Experimental and fitted Nyquist plots of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G in the alkaline electrolyte.

(a) CV and (b) LSV curves of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G. (c) n and HO2 − yield calculated through RRDE measurements. (d) Current-time chronoamperometric measurement of the B–N-G in the neutral electrolyte.

(a) Illustration of operando FTIR cell with external reflection configuration. (b, c) Operando FTIR spectra with the LSV curve of the B–N-G in 0.1 M KOH. (d) Structure and charge density distribution configuration of the B–N coordinated active site (brown: C, green: B, white N, pink: H and yellow and cyan areas corresponding to the charge density increase and decrease area, respectively.). (e) Top view configurations of optimized structure after adsorption of ORR intermediates on B–N coordinated active site. (f) Free energy diagram of B–N graphene at different potentials. (g) DOS of graphene and B–N graphene. (h) PDOS of B–N graphene. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Illustration of preparation of the integrated B–N-G air cathode and Al-air battery. (b) Current-voltage and power density curves, (c) discharge curves at different current densities of Al-air batteries with the integrated B–N-G air cathode, slurry cast B–N-G and Pt/C air cathode. (d) Long-term discharge curves of Al-air batteries with the integrated B–N-G air cathode in a 4 M NaOH electrolyte. (e) Current-voltage and power density curves, (f) discharge curves at different current densities of Al-air batteries with integrated B–N-G air cathode, slurry cast B–N-G and Pt/C air cathode. (g) Long-term discharge curves of Al-air batteries with the integrated B–N-G air cathode air cathode in a 3.5% NaCl electrolyte.

head.ce:title: Rational design of boron-nitrogen coordinated active sites towards oxygen reduction reaction in aluminum-air batteries with robust integrated air cathode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of cost-effective electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is essential for developing metal-air batteries. In this work, we propose a pre-self-assembled strategy to prepare controllable B–N coordinated active sites anchored on carbon supports as an efficient electrocatalyst for ORR. The precisely coordinated B–N coordination from the pre-self-assembled boronate-amino ligands are sealed on the in-situ formed carbon dots (CDs) for preservation and further converted into B–N coordinated active sites on the carbon support. The B–N coordinated active sites anchored on the graphene aerogel (denoted as B–N-G) exhibit Pt/C competitive ORR catalytic activity in both alkaline and neutral media, which are attributed to the high content of efficient B–N coordinated active sites with expanded lattice spacing. The operando Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and density functional theory (DFT) calculations explain the catalytic mechanism and identify the active sites of the B–N coordination. Furthermore, the B–N-G is directly processed into an integrated air cathode, and the Al-air batteries with the B–N-G integrated air cathode exhibit superior discharge performance and good stability in both NaOH and NaCl electrolytes. This study demonstrates a feasible pre-self-assembled approach to achieve designed B–N coordination in carbon-based electrocatalysts for metal-air batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-air batteries have been highlighted as next-generation energy storage systems. Among them, aluminum-air (Al-air) batteries are promising and efficient power sources due to the high energy density, good safety, and abundant reserves, low cost and light weight of aluminum [1,2]. However, the sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) on air cathode greatly limits power density of Al-air batteries for practical application [3,4]. Therefore, electrocatalysts are required to lower the reaction energy barrier of ORR for improving the performance of Al-air batteries [5,6]. Platinum-based materials are currently the most efficient ORR electrocatalysts [7,8]. However, the scarcity and high cost of Pt significantly restrain their large-scale commercial application, and the stability of Pt-based ORR electrocatalysts are also unsatisfactory [9]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop low-cost non-noble metal ORR electrocatalysts. Currently, various non-noble metal ORR electrocatalysts including transition metals and carbon-based materials have been widely investigated [6,10–12]. Among them, carbon-based materials receive great interest because of low cost, environmental friendliness, and large-scale availability of raw materials [13]. However, the ORR catalytic activity of carbon-based materials is still unsatisfactory. Strategies including doping metallic or non-metallic heteroatoms and introducing intrinsic/topologic vacancies in carbon-based materials have been reported for creating efficient active sites and lowering reaction barriers for ORR [14–17].

The doped non-metallic heteroatoms (e.g., B, N, O, P, S) in carbon matrix are functioned as the active sites for ORR due to the regulated electronic structure and optimized adsorption energies of oxygen intermediates [18,19]. Compared with mono-heteroatom doping, dual-heteroatom doping is more effective for creating active sites with higher catalytic activities and lower ORR energy barriers [20,21]. However, the multiple heteroatoms generally are randomly doped in carbon matrix with a disordered manner [22], which brings great difficulty in determining the configuration of active sites and analyzing the synergetic effect between the different heteroatoms. It is therefore necessary to synthesize designed and controllable coordination active sites in carbon-based electrocatalysts. Selectively introducing specific moieties or ligands with designed coordination in precursors is an efficient way for synthesizing doped carbon catalysts with controllable active sites [22,23]. Among doped metal-free carbon materials, boron and nitrogen doped carbon have been widely investigated [24,25]. Unfortunately, carbon materials with coordinated B–N dual-heteroatoms active sites have seldom been investigated due to the challenge in designing appropriate B–N coordination. Therefore, it is necessary to develop controllable strategy that can form ligands with precise B–N coordination to achieve the targeted B–N coordinated active sites in carbon matrix. Pre-forming strong intermolecular boron-nitrogen coordination through self-assembling boric acid and amine functional groups may be beneficial for creating B–N coordinated active sites in the carbon matrix.

In this work, we propose a pre-self-assembled strategy to prepare controllable B–N coordinated active sites anchored on graphene aerogel as both efficient electrocatalyst for ORR and robust integrated air cathode for Al-air batteries. The boronate-amino ligands are pre-self-assembled and then sealed on the in-situ formed carbon dots (CDs), and these boronate-amino ligands on CDs can be preserved and converted into B–N coordinated active sites on the graphene aerogel support (denoted as B–N-G) during the thermal treatment. Benefiting from the high intrinsic catalytic activity of B–N coordinated active sites and the enlarged lattice spacing, the as-prepared B–N-G exhibits much better ORR performance than its counterparts with disorderly/separately distributed B, N heteroatoms in both alkaline and neutral electrolytes. The reaction mechanism of ORR on B–N coordinated active sites is also studied through operando Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, and the B–N coordination with a lower energy barrier is identified as the main active sites for ORR. Furthermore, the alkaline and neutral Al-air batteries with B–N-G integrated air cathode displays better performance than alkaline and neutral Al-air batteries with B–N-G and Pt/C slurry cast air cathodes, demonstrating good practical application.

Firstly, 0.44 g of urea and 0.22 g of 4-vinylphenylboronic acid were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and stirred for 4 h. Then, 1.07 g of citric acid was added and stirred for another 4 h (denoted as solution A). Meanwhile, 20 mg of GO was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 4 h (denoted as solution B). Then, solution B was slowly added into solution A with stirring. The mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained aerogel after hydrothermal treatment was washed with deionized water to remove the soluble impurity and was freeze-dried for 24 h. Finally, the dried sample was pyrolyzed at 1000 °C for 3 h with a ramp of 5 °C min−1 to obtain the B–N-G.

For comparison, the B doped graphene (B-G) was prepared through the identical procedure but without adding 4-vinylphenylboronic, and N doped graphene (N-G) was prepared through the identical procedure but without adding urea. The B doped and N doped graphene (B-G/N-G) was prepared through the identical procedure but without adding citric acid.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, RISE-MAGNA) and transmission electron microscope, (TEM, Talos F200X G2) were used to obtain the morphology information. Near edge X-ray absorption fine structure (NEXAFS) spectra were obtained using soft X-rays at the beamline BL02B02 at Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF). X-ray diffraction patterns were measured on a D8 ADVANCE Da Vinci X-ray diffractometer. Solid state and operando FTIR were conducted on a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer using KBr pressing method and attenuated total reflectance (ATR) method, respectively. nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were obtained using 600 MHz solid state NMR spectrometer (*AVANCE NEO). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS UltraDLD). Raman spectra were obtained utilizing a 532 nm incident radiation on a confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Qontor). Information on the specific surface area and porosity were acquired through nitrogen adsorption-desorption tests using an Autosorb-IQ3 BET analyzer.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with three-electrode configuration on potentiostat (Gamry Ref 600+), and LSV curves were obtained through cathodic scanning. The catalyst ink was prepared by dispersing 2 mg of catalyst in 480 μL of ethanol and 20 μL of binder (5% Nafion) with ultrasonication. Subsequently, 7.5 μL of ink was deposited on a polished glassy carbon electrode with a diameter of 5 mm (mass loading: ∼153 μg cm−2), while graphite rod and saturated calomel electrode were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The used electrolyte is either oxygen saturated 0.1 M KOH or 3.5% NaCl solution. Commercially available Pt/C composed of 20 wt% Pt and 80 wt% carbon black (Macklin Co. Ltd, Shanghai) was used as a benchmark for ORR performance comparison. The method for converting potentials against reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) follows our previous work [20]. All electrochemical tests were conducted at 25 ± 1 °C. Furthermore, the detailed calculations of electron transfer number through Koutecky-Levich (K-L) equation and RRDE measurement are provided in the supporting information.

The Al-air cell was home-made battery with a size of 3*3*3 cm3. The integrated air cathode with a thickness of ∼0.5 mm was prepared by directly pressing the B–N-G onto a Ni foam current collector. The slurry cast air cathode was prepared by mixing B–N-G, Vulcan xc-72 and poly-tetrafluoroethylene (60 wt% emulsion) with a mass ratio of 1:3:1 in ethanol, which was then formed a paste and rolled onto a Ni foam current collector. The anode was Al-1.0 wt% Mg-0.1 wt% Sn (Al–Mg–Sn) alloy. The electrolyte was either 4 M NaOH solution or 3.5% NaCl solution (∼30 mL). The discharge testing of Al-air batteries were conducted on a LAND testing system (LAND Electronics Ltd.).
DFT calculation in this work was implemented using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) code, the simulation details are provided in the supporting information.

The synthesis of the B–N-G with B–N coordinated active sites is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, 4-vinylphenylboronic acid and urea were self-assembled to form the boronate-amino ligands, which is confirmed by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1). During the subsequent hydrothermal treatment, the formed boronate-amino ligands and critic acid were polymerized to form carbon dots, and the B–N coordination was sealed on the carbon dots. The formed carbon dots with a diameter of ∼5 nm are evidenced by the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the carbon dots with multi-functional groups were firmly anchored on the graphene oxide sheets to form the carbon dots (CDs) modified graphene oxide aerogel (denoted as CDs-GO) after the hydrothermal process. The hydrothermal treatment here can activate the in-situ polymerization of boronate-amino ligands and critic acid to form carbon dots with B–N coordination and anchor these in-situ formed carbon dots on the surface of the GO to prevent from aggregation. Moreover, the GO sheets were reduced to form reduced graphene oxide aerogel with free-standing structure during hydrothermal treatment. The CDs anchored on the graphene oxide sheet are clearly observed by the TEM and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscope (HADDF-STEM) images (Figs. S3 and S4). Therefore, the boronate-amino ligands were uniformly distributed on the graphene oxide sheets, which are supported by the element mappings in Fig. S5. Lastly, the CDs-GO was pyrolyzed to obtain the B–N-G. Due to the precise coordination of boronate-amino ligands on the CDs, the B–N coordination was inherited in the B–N-G aerogel. For comparison, the B-G/N-G was also prepared without adding the CDs carriers (Fig. S6). Without the pre-formed boronate-amino ligands sealed on the CDs, the B–N-G cannot be formed, as the formed CDs can preserve the B–N coordination from boronate-amino ligands. Furthermore, graphene aerogel with B active sites or N active sites was synthesized without adding N source or B source (denoted as B-G or N-G) for comparison, respectively.

Raman spectra of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G in Fig. S7 display the G band at ∼1585 cm−1 and D band ∼1345 cm−1, corresponding to graphitic and disordered carbon, respectively [26]. Among them, the B–N-G possesses the highest value of I D /I G (1.27), suggesting the most abundant defects in the carbon matrix created by the doped heteroatoms [27]. The K-edge NEXAFS spectra of B in Fig. 1 a displays that the B–N-G exhibits a strong peak at 194.0 eV corresponding to the transition of B 1s electrons to the unoccupied B 2p (π*) states with graphitized B–N structure, while the B-G/N-G only exhibits a weak peak at 194.4 eV corresponding to the transition of B 1s electrons to the unoccupied B 2p (π*) states with B–O bond [28]. This strong peak intensity reveals that there is a high B–N content in the B–N-G. XPS analysis was used to determine surface chemistry of the various B and N doped graphene. The survey scans confirm the existence of C, B, and N in the B–N-G and B-G/N-G (Fig. S8). The C 1s spectra of the B–N-G and B-G/N-G were deconvoluted into five peaks at ∼283.9, 284.7, 285.2, 286.2 and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C–B, C–C, C–N, C–O and O–CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S9) [29], and the C–B bond or C–N bond is observed in the spectrum of the B-G or N-G, respectively (Fig. S9). The intensity ratio of C–N + C–B to C–C can be used to determine the defect created by heteroatoms, and the intensity ratio of C–N + C–B to C–C value of the B–N-G (1.14) is higher than that of the B-G (0.04), N-G (0.49) and B-G/N-G (0.99), indicating the B–N-G has more heteroatoms created non-regular hexagonal defective carbon rings. The N 1s spectra of the B–N-G, N-G and B-G/N-G were deconvoluted at 398.2, 399.3401.2 and 403.2 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and oxidized N, respectively (Fig. 1b and Fig. S10) [30]. Likewise, the B 1s spectrum of the B–N-G was deconvoluted to four peaks at 189.6, 190.8, 191.9 and 193.0 eV, which are attributed to B–C, B–N, BC2O and BCO2 species, respectively (Fig. 1c) [31,32], while the B 1s spectra of other samples are provided in Fig. S11. As a result, the fractions of all B and N species in the B–N-G and B-G/N-G are provided in Fig. 1d, while the B and N fractions of all samples are shown in Table S1. The B–N-G exhibits higher contents of pyridinic and pyrrolic N than the B-G/N-G, and these two kinds of N species are considered as the most active sites for ORR [33]. Moreover, the B–N-G possesses not only a higher content of heteroatoms but also a higher content of B–N specie than the B-G/N-G. The 11B solid-state NMR spectra of the B–N-G and B-G are shown in Fig. 1e, the B–N-G exhibits a peak at ∼12.4 ppm, while the B-G exhibits a peak at ∼10.0 ppm. The peak shift after the introduction of N is attributed to the formation of B–N coordination [34]. Furthermore, the FTIR was used to confirm the predominant B–N active sites in the B–N-G, and the FTIR spectrum of B–N-G displays a peak at ∼760 cm−1, which is ascribed to the out-of-plane B–N bending (Fig. 1f) [35,36]. These results clearly identify the B–N coordination in the B–N-G because the boronate-amino ligands are sealed on the CDs to retain the B–N coordination in the B–N-G.

The SEM images exhibit the uniformly distributed graphene sheets in the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S12), and the TEM images further confirm the flat sheet morphology of these samples (Fig. 2b and Fig. S13). The HADDF-STEM image with element mappings of the B–N-G display that B and N elements are homogeneous distributed in the carbon matrix (Fig. 2c–f), indicating the possible doping of heteroatoms through the CDs assisted synthesis. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the B–N-G and B-G/N-G display the H3-type curves, revealing the mesoporous structure (Fig. S14a) [20]. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and average pore volume of the B–N-G are 252.1 m2 g−1 and 1.128 cc g−1, respectively, while those are 167.9 m2 g−1 and 1.002 cc g−1 for B-G/N-G. Moreover, the pore size distribution plots reveal that the pores in B–N-G and B-G/N-G mainly consist of mesopores with a diameter of ∼4.0 nm (Fig. S14b). Due to the additional mesoporous structure derived from the CDs, the total specific surface area and pore volume of the B–N-G are larger than those of the B-G/N-G [20,37]. Moreover, the crystalline structure of the B–N-G is confirmed by the HRTEM image with a lattice spacing of 0.366 nm (Fig. 2g) and the inserted Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern with spot lattices of (002) and (101) graphene planes. Interestingly, the lattice spacing of the B–N-G is different from that of the B-G (0.359 nm), N-G (0.344 nm) and B-G/N-G (0.348 nm) (Fig. S15). XRD patterns of the B-G, N-G, B-G/N-G, and B–N-G display two obvious peaks at ∼26° and ∼44°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) crystal planes, respectively (Fig. 2h). The intensity ratios of (002) peak to (101) peak (I 002 /I 101) were calculated, and the B–N-G exhibits the lowest value of I 002 /I 101 (6.33), suggesting that the anchored CDs were transformed into an additional crystalline carbon layer on graphene after pyrolysis [38]. Furthermore, Fig. 2i displays that the (002) peak of the B–N-G shifts to the lowest angle (25.83°), further demonstrating the largest lattice spacing. The largest lattice spacing of the B–N-G can be explained as follow: the bond lengths of the formed B–C (1.56 Å), N–C (1.48 Å) bond and B–N bond (1.57 Å) are extended compared with the C–C bond (1.42 Å) (Fig. 2j) [39–42], and the heteroatom will be alleviated from the basal plane of the graphited carbon matrix due to the extended bond length. The theoretically calculated alleviated height of the B–N-G model is larger than 0.670 Å, while those of B-G model and N-G model are 0.417 Å and 0.646 Å, respectively (Fig. 2k and Fig. S16) [43]. Namely, the formation of B–N coordination in B–N-G can enlarge the lattice spacing.

CV curves of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G display obvious reduction peaks in the oxygen-saturated alkaline electrolyte (Fig. 3 a). The B–N-G exhibits the most positive peak potential (E p) of 0.817 V, indicating the ORR on B–N-G is the most thermodynamic favorable in the alkaline electrolyte [44,45]. Fig. 3b shows the LSV polarization curves of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G. The E onset and E 1/2 of these samples in the alkaline electrolyte extracted from the LSV curves are provided in Table S2. The B–N-G exhibits the most positive onset potential (E onset) of 0.960 V and half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.868 V, which are comparable to those of the Pt/C (E onset = 0.983 V and E 1/2 = 0.854 V). The result reveals the high ORR activity of the B–N-G, which can be attributed to the rich B–N coordinated active sites in the B–N-G. The fitted Tafel slopes demonstrate that the B–N-G possesses the smallest Tafel slope (62.3 dec mV−1) (Fig. 3c). Additionally, the LSV curve of the B-G + N-G (mechanically mixing B-G with N-G with a mass ratio of 1:1) is also provided in Fig. S17 for comparison. The E 1/2 of the B-G + N-G (0.694 V) is much negative than that of the B–N-G, further demonstrating the superior ORR catalytic activity of the B–N coordinated active sites. LSV curves with different rotating speeds and the corresponding Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plots of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G are given to calculate the electron transfer number (Figs. S18 and S19). The calculated electron transfer number (n) of the B–N-G is 3.88, which is larger than 2.96, 3.28 and 3.49 for the B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G, respectively. Rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) measurements were tested to measure the n and byproduct (HO2 −) yield of all the samples. The B–N-G exhibits an average n of 3.82, and the HO2 − yield is ∼9% (Fig. 3d and Fig. S20), which further confirms the four-electron reaction pathway during ORR in the alkaline electrolyte. Furthermore, the durability of the B–N-G was evaluated, and the attenuation of E 1/2 is ∼3 mV after 10000 cycles (Fig. 3e), indicating the good ORR stability of the B–N-G. Moreover, the chronoamperometric curve displays that the current remains 94.1% of its initial current after 15000 s (Fig. 3f), also revealing the good stability of the B–N-G. Additionally, the ORR performance of the B–N-G in the alkaline electrolyte was compared with other B, N doped electrocatalysts reported in literature (Table S3), and the E onset and E 1/2 of the B–N-G are the most competitive among them. The results above clearly demonstrate that the B–N-G shows superior ORR performance, which is ascribed to the high activity of the B–N active sites.

The origin of catalytic activity of the B–N-G in the alkaline electrolyte was identified. The specific kinetic current densities normalized to mass (j K, m) and ECSA (j K, ECSA) were calculated (see details in supporting information, Fig. S21). The specific electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was also obtained by calculating the double-layer capacitance (C dl) (Fig. S22), and the highest C dl of the B–N-G (542.4 μF) in Fig. 3g is ascribed to the abundant exposed active sites in the alkaline electrolyte. The B–N-G shows the largest j K, m (25.9 mA mg−1) and ECSA (903.3 cm2 mg−1), revealing the highest activity (Fig. S23) [46–48]. Meanwhile, the B–N-G shows a much larger j K, ECSA value (0.029 mA cm−2) than the counterparts of B-G (0.010 mA cm−2), N-G (0.011 mA cm−2) and B-G/N-G (0.024 mA cm−2) (Fig. 3h). The largest j K, ECSA of the B–N-G reveals the highest intrinsic activity of the B–N coordination towards ORR. In addition, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to evaluate the charge-transfer. The experimental and fitted Nyquist plots of these samples are provided in Fig. 3i, and the equivalent circuit for fitting is shown in Fig. S24 with the output data in Table S4. The fitting results demonstrate that the B–N-G exhibits the lowest charge transfer resistance (R ct) of 3.9 Ω, suggesting the B–N coordination can accelerate the charge transfer.

The ORR performance of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G in a NaCl (3.5%) electrolyte was also evaluated. CV curves of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G display obvious reduction peaks in the oxygen-saturated neutral electrolyte (Fig. 4 a). Likewise, the B–N-G exhibits the most positive E p at 0.492 V, suggesting the ORR on B–N-G is the most thermodynamic favorable. LSV polarization curves of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G are shown in Fig. 4b. The E onset and E 1/2 of these samples in the neutral electrolyte extracted from the LSV curves are provided in Table S5. The B–N-G shows the most positive E onset (0.702 V) and E 1/2 (0.559 V) among all samples, suggesting the best catalytic performance in the neutral electrolyte. Similarly, LSV curves with different rotating speeds and corresponded K-L plots of the B–N-G, B-G, N-G, and B-G/N-G are shown in Figs. S25 and S26. The calculated n of the B–N-G is 3.77, which is larger than 2.62, 3.21 and 3.45 for the B-G, N-G and B-G/N-G, respectively. Moreover, the B–N-G exhibits an average n of 3.80, and the HO2 − yield of ∼10% (Fig. 4c and Fig. S27), which further confirm the four-electron reaction pathway during ORR in the neutral electrolyte. The long-term cycle measurement reveals that the loss of E 1/2 is only 5 mV after 10000 CV cycles (Fig. S28), indicating the good stability of the B–N-G in the neutral electrolyte. Likewise, the chronoamperometric curves shows the current retains 90.7% of its initial value after 15000 s (Fig. 4d), further revealing good stability of the B–N-G in the neutral electrolyte.

Based on the electrochemical analysis, the best ORR catalytic performance of the B–N-G in both alkaline and neutral electrolytes can be attributed to the rational design of the B–N coordination in the graphene aerogel. The B–N coordination can not only boost the intrinsic activity but also provide more active sites for the ORR.

To illustrate the mechanism of ORR on the B–N coordinated active sites, operando FTIR was conducted with a homemade operando FTIR cell. To minimize the IR absorption in the aqueous electrolyte, the in-situ FTIR spectra were collected in an external reflection configuration with attenuated total reflection infrared (ATR-IR) mode (Fig. 5 a) [49]. The operando FTIR spectra of the B–N-G operated at different potentials are shown in Fig. 5b and c. At an initial potential (point A), the oxygen molecules were adsorbed on the surface of the B–N-G, and the peak at ∼ 1150 cm−1 is attributed to the B–O in-plane bending [50], which is explained by the adsorbed O2 on the B–N coordinated active site. As the potential negatively shifted to point B, the oxygen reduction reaction began to occur, and the adsorbed O2 was dissociated, thus the peak at ∼1150 cm−1 disappears. Meanwhile, an obvious B–OH bending mode peak at ∼1630 cm−1 appears [51], suggesting that the O2 reacted with water to form the intermediates. Subsequently, with negatively shifting the potential to point C and D, the intensity of the B–OH bending mode peak decreases. This is explained that as the employed potential negatively shifted, more and more OH− ions generated and dissociated from the electrode leading to the significant change in concentration of OH specie on the surface of the B–N-G [52]. The results evidence that the B–N coordination is the main active site for the ORR.

The structure and charge density distribution configuration of the B–N coordination active site on graphene model (denoted as B–N graphene) are shown in Fig. 5d. It can be clearly observed that B atom is the electron donor in the B–N active site, therefore it is positively charged and more favorable for the adsorption of negatively charged intermediates. Moreover, due to the higher electronegativity of N compared with C, it possesses greater ability to polarize the adjacent B atom and make it lose more charges further facilitating the ORR intermediate adsorption [18]. Top view configurations of the optimized structure of the B–N coordination active site after adsorption of O2*, OOH*, O* and OH* intermediates are provided in Fig. 5e, and the corresponding side view configurations are provided in Fig. S29. The calculated binding free energies of O2*, OOH*, O* and OH* on B–N coordination active site are 5.50, 4.63, 2.16 and 1.35 eV, respectively. The Gibbs free energy diagram of B–N at zero potential (U = 0), equilibrium potential (U = 1.23 V), and limiting potential (the potential which maintains all elementary reactions exothermic) are given in Fig. 5f. The non-electron transfer reaction step (O2 to O2*) is determined by oxygen coverage, and this dissociation barrier can be reduced without exerting external potential [53]. All steps can occur spontaneously at U = 0 V initially and gradually become less exothermic as the external potential increases. When U = 1.23 V, the applied external voltage (ΔE) to activate ORR is zero (ΔE = 1.23-U), therefore most of the elementary reactions is thermodynamically unfavorable. The limiting potential was calculated to be 0.85 V, which is close to the measured half-wave potential. The curves of density of states (DOS) of the graphene and B–N graphene are shown in Fig. 5g, and the B–N graphene exhibits higher DOS than pure graphene near Fermi level, indicating B–N coordination active site can provide better electron mobility for ORR [54]. Fig. 5h is the projected density of states (PDOS) plot of B–N graphene. The formation of B–N coordination can move the B2p and total orbital closer to the Fermi level and further improve the electron occupancy state of B–N graphene, thus boosting the oxygen absorption efficiency to accelerate the oxygen reaction kinetic [55].

Al-air batteries with alkaline and neutral electrolytes were assembled to explore the practical application of the B–N-G. Fig. 6 a illustrates that the catalyst can be directly processed to form the integrated air cathode without any addition of binder or conductive enhancer, which can not only simply the processing but also reduce the inner resistance. Fig. 6b is the current-voltage and power density curves of the Al-air batteries with the integrated B–N-G, slurry cast B–N-G and Pt/C air cathodes in a 4 M NaOH electrolyte. The voltage of Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode is higher than those with the slurry cast B–N-G and Pt/C air cathode. Moreover, the maximum power density of the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode is 152.4 mW cm−2, which is larger than those with the slurry cast B–N-G (137.1 mW cm−2) and Pt/C (136.2 mW cm−2) air cathodes. The results reveal the superior ORR catalytic activity of the B–N-G and merit of integrated electrode. Fig. 6c shows that the discharge voltage plateaus of the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode are larger than those of the counterpart at different current densities (5, 20, 50 and 100 mA cm−2) in the alkaline electrolyte. Moreover, the voltage recovers to 1.71 V when the current density goes back to 5 mA cm−2, demonstrating robust structure maintenance of the B–N-G during discharging. Fig. 6d is the long-term discharge curve of the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode at a current density of 20 mA cm−2 with mechanical charging every 4 h. The voltage plateau remains at ∼1.58 V and there is no voltage loss after continuous mechanical charge. Then the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode was evaluated after off-load storage of 5 days in contact with KOH electrolyte and dissolved aluminum species, and the discharge voltage remains at ∼1.55 V, suggesting the good durability of the integrated B–N-G air cathode in the alkaline electrolyte.

Likewise, the performance of Al-air battery with B–N-G in the neutral electrolyte was studied. The Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode displays higher discharge voltages and power densities than those with slurry cast and commercial Pt/C in the neutral electrolyte (Fig. 6e). The maximum power density of the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode is 20.2 mW cm−2, which is also higher than those with the slurry cast B–N-G (17.5 mW cm−2) and Pt/C (19.1 mW cm−2) air cathode. Fig. 6f shows that the working voltages of the Al-air battery with the integrated B–N-G air cathode are higher than those with slurry cast B–N-G air cathode at different current densities. The corresponding long-term discharge curve demonstrates that the voltage maintains a 1.13 V after mechanical charging every 4 h. Then again, the off-load storage of 5 days in contact with NaCl electrolyte and dissolved aluminum species was also employed, and the discharge voltage can still remain at ∼1.09 V (Fig. 6g), suggesting the good durability of the integrated B–N-G air cathode in the neutral electrolyte.

In summary, the controllable B–N coordinated active sites anchored on graphene aerogel are prepared through a pre-self-assembled strategy followed by thermal treatment, in which the in-situ formed CDs can seal and preserve the pre-self-assembled B–N coordination. The B–N coordination in the B–N-G is confirmed by XPS, FTIR and NMR analyses. The B–N-G exhibits competitive ORR performance Pt/C in both alkaline and neutral electrolytes. The superior ORR performance is attributed to the intrinsically high activity of the B–N coordinated active sites, which is confirmed through the analysis of ECSA and j K, ECSA. The ORR mechanism on the B–N coordinated active sites is revealed by operando FTIR measurement and DFT calculation, and the B–N coordination with a low reaction energy barrier is confirmed as main active sites for ORR. Furthermore, the alkaline and neutral Al-air batteries with the integrated B–N-G air cathode display larger power densities than that with the slurry cast B–N-G and Pt/C air cathodes and great durability, indicating promising potential of Al-air batteries for practical application.

Ruiqi Cheng: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material synthesis, ORR measurements, Conducted the Al-air battery test, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Kaiqi Li: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material synthesis, ORR measurements, Conducted the Al-air battery test, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Zhao Li: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material characterization, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Min Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material synthesis, ORR measurements, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Fei Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the Al-air battery test, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Zhaohui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material characterization. Tianshuo Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material characterization, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript. Pengyu Meng: Writing – review & editing, Conducted the material characterization. Chaopeng Fu: Conceived the idea, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Directed the research, All authors discussed the results and edited the manuscript.
